segments.text: Hi everyone. Welcome to our presentation about how to get from plans and point clouds to
have already reached wins with APS and ACC.
Here is our team. This is our program.
And let's start with introduction. My name is Martin. I'm founder and managing director
at IOLabs. I'm expert in digital planning and implementation of complex projects and customer
requirements. I'm also teaching as a doctor at Hoxheul, Zeren in Switzerland.
We are a team of 15 specialists on the 2018 and we focus on custom software for architecture
engineering and construction and programmatic data management. Everything we do based on our
proprietary IOL framework called IOL framework and we have also one another proprietary solution
called IO-Sync which works perfectly for data management programmatic data management operations.
We are certified Autodesk forage or APS partners since many years. I think it was 2018 already.
We are as certified system integrator and of course Siemens partner.
As I said, our three main fields of our activities are custom software development for architecture
engineering and construction. We work closely in GIS, BIM and digital twin field and we focus on
programmatic data management with IOLsync. Hello. My name is Aros Laptainiček. I'm chief
production officer at ILOPS AG and I'm in charge of delivering the IOM product. I also
actively participate on its development and I work in ILOPS since 2020 and I'm also working with
the Autodesk APS since 2016. I'm just taking that word. Hello. Sven Eichshapka speaking here.
I'm from the Oksalja Westcom group and Autodesk Platinum partner. Over 25 years in the BIM space,
over 10 years in research looking at early AI applications, process engineering and also BIM
obviously and then working for Think Project and Lock Lab as part of Hexagon Group for over 10 years.
In software development, business development all around the digitization in our AC space.
Throughout the years, I've been also quite actively being in standardization at ISO 19650,
the multi model container and stuff like that that I did in the past. So I've been around for quite
a while and I have the privilege to start off with a few slides to introduce the collaboration we
have with ILOPS and the IOM product and the headlines for this one is the minimal BIM approach for us.
And not surprisingly, if we look at the space of asset and facility management,
as in all industries and areas, obviously we have a rising demand. Operational and maintenance costs
are quite a factor, have become a factor. Outsourcing is not the solution anymore. We have a lot of
increase in reporting for ESG. We have high demands for our resilience in the operations. And at the
same time, we have shortage of people and expertise. Our data systems are quite distributed. So we
have silos of data. The data in them is even if it's in one system, quite often,
interconnected, incomplete. It's missing the spatial context. And at the same time, obviously,
we have quite a lot of technical advances. We have new AI solutions. We have automations
that kind of gives us the feeling that we can solve all these problems integrating that data,
advancing the data. However, the main challenge that both Martin experiences with all his customers
that we experience with our customer is the lack of data or foundation that we have. In any project,
we have all three sources. It's either old analog plans. It's maybe advanced BIM models or it's
scans and point clouds. But all each of these sources bring their problems. Obviously,
plans are incomplete. We need to analyze them. We have to put them into context. We have to
collect the data into a link it. BIM models are quite often advanced, but they're expensive.
They have a lot of information that we may not need for the operations pace. And at the same time,
then we have wonderful point clouds, large data sets. But obviously, there is semantically,
not that rich. They're quite poor, actually. And we need to bring it all together.
And while over the years, we have looked at particularly BIM to FM approaches, which work for
quite fine. But in the end, in the acid and facility management model, what we need is roughly 10%
of the data we had in the BIM space. So what we are looking at this is why try to invent the
real and make the big solution. Let's keep it really simple. Let's start with a spatial model.
That is fast, that is cost effective, that can be automatically generated from drawings,
that can be automatically generated from point clouds and other scans. And the good thing,
it is at the same time scalable. So there is no need to stay on spatial systems. We can always
uplift these models and generate more detail, advance them, add data to these spatial models.
So looking at these minimal BIM approach, we recognize that in the start of a lot of new projects,
it's easy to start. We're getting a lot of traction and we're getting the systems into the business.
The good thing is, and obviously why I'm talking about this minimal BIM approach is,
ION apps has created a wonderful suite of solutions with IORM, that takes all these different data
inputs, provides services, automated services for hybrid modeling, using automated technology, AI
technology, whatever is there and whatever needs to be done to produce these central models,
and from very simple minimal models, then we are integrating, we're providing IORM with a lot
of building and applications, that we will show you in quite a minute, a lot of geometry, simple
geometry, but all the needed attributes and open APIs to integrate these models and applications
with any of your operational systems that you have. So now let's take a look at how this IORM model
actually has been created. It's very simple. Our customers can actually upload us any files they
have, it can be floor plans and or point clouds. There are of course fewer requirements.
If there is a stamp, it has to be reliable. The point cloud has to be consistent, but besides
of that, which is logical, we can work with basically anything. After the client drops the data,
we can analyze it with our scripts and our experts create and quote for the client and after the
client accepts it, he gets a model. He can either be happy just with the model and use it for
his workflows or he can take the model and use it in our app, which gives him a lot of additional
features. Now let's take a look at it, how it works in detail. So the key effect is how the model is
created. So we have several aspects. We have the point cloud information, which gives us information
how the model actually looks like. We have the floor plans, which gives us the information how the
room was planned. And we have the exact geometry, which gives us the information about areas.
How big is my room? Which is the most important part for facility management. And those rooms
have also information. They have a room code and they know to which floor they belong to
which building, to which site and to which project. And knowing that, we have a like solid base
for structured data and reached with point cloud, yet like also very cheap, like compute wise
context. And we can start working with it. We can simply create issues if something is broken or
assets, if something is worth tracking. And this is basically the core of our iLIFM solution.
Some of the key features are space management. So we can basically give our customers exact
overview of their portfolio. For me, it was quite shocking when I figured out that especially
large customers from the public sector doesn't actually know what they have, what they are responsible for.
But when I got deeper into it, I understood the problem. They have often very old, even
land-grown plants. They have from some projects, DWGs and some projects are being reconstructed.
And nobody knows where is actually the last version of the documentation. And it creates a perfect
chaos. So we help with that. And as I said, if we have the model, we can even erase it. We can
create objects or identify objects which are worth tracking those call assets. We can create them,
place them, manage and work with them, which is basically the core of the facility management
together with the space management. The next important thing is issue reporting. So if something
is broken, we can report it or the user can report it and we have a system which creates a
single source of truth. And our iLIFM app can streamline it into different subsystems.
What is really cool and one of the newest features is that we have created the MCP server for our
iLIFM solution and it gives us a perfect connection of opportunities. So basically the user can
even report in some other software that he was something that broke. And for example,
chair has one broken leg. He can say it. The MCP server gets the information and he creates
issue for the user in iLIFM which is accessible through the whole system.
The second but maybe the most important thing is the sensor indication. So if we have those
simply created models, we can also streamline the sensor data which gives our customers perfect
base for ESG and environmental social governance. And this is one of the crucial points in the project
because if our users can track their building, they can also prove that the measures they are doing
contribute to better performance which to clients want.
So the use cases which iLIFM is bringing is virtual walkthrough which is actually one of the
points would really save costs because if somebody wants to plan a repair, technicians can take a
look in the room before they go inside and they can prepare the tools for the reparation.
Besides of many other use cases, I think this is super helpful for the users.
We provide as build versus as designed comparatively. So if we compare the three ESG ends with
actually floor plans, we can see the differences. And this gives us our plans also the opportunity to
see how the building actually was built in compared to what was planned. The ESG is really important
and it will be more important in the future. We are actually focusing on the like a medium-sized
clients, let's say a company with one or let's say few factories or property manager with let's say
from 20 to let's say 60 apartments like a average medium user. And we aim for them to give them
very accessible and super useful digital points. What is also very important for us are integrations
in other systems. So we have integration in EARP, namely as APU in the very and Microsoft Dynamics
in the very advanced stages. And our open API enables broad range of integrations. And as I said
before, our IAFM provides also an MCP server. So and now take a look, let's take a look at that
application and to represent it from your sluff. So we have started development of IAFM class
all time. What you can see here now is the dashboard. It's nothing fancy but you can maintain the
basic overview of all of your project. You can see them on azirimuth. Each of those project is
geolocated and you can also see which projects are currently being modeled. And from this from this
point, you can navigate further. So everything starts with creating a project and here we have to
tackle multiple things. First one is the schemes. You can see on the screenshot that we have
location schemes, issue schemes, asset schemes, element schemes. And these basically serve as a
templates for how you want your projects to function. It also determines the higher, for instance,
of locations. You can define everything within the IAFM. And using this, you can ensure that all
of your projects will behave the same way. It's almost the similar as ACC project templates.
So here we can see the model of project Montessori, Schuhl. And here we see the
combined view of an Autodesk viewer with the rumor list.
User can define his own custom attributes here and edit the values. Of course the IFC
originally imported values are not editable. You can also see the column code SIA 416, which we can
use to visually color all the rooms. So you can get some semantic coloring and seeing the
buildings in a different context. And as well, if you change those values, you can export the IFC
back with all of your changed information. Here we can see that inside the model, we can also enable
a mesh layer from a FBX file. And for that we are using those three buttons on each row of the room.
So we can on demand, like load a basically a room view and using a button we can switch
to a view from inside the room. We don't have a screenshot for that in here, but trust me on that.
And as well, here we can see the room coloring and the tooltits, which can be really helpful to
navigate between different parts of the building to see them in the specialised disciplines that
you can also define on your own. You can define the special types of attributes that could
color your model in your own way as you need for classifying, for instance, based on which
company owns which offices, etc. On this screen we can see the Assets view, which is again
the split view. So you can visualize how your assets are located across your building. And on the
next slide we can see the detail of an asset which exactly uses this FBX layer, the mesh view from
inside the room. And then the asset is placed exactly on the chair. Basically the chair is the asset.
You can, it's very similar to ACC, but with that difference that we can use the push pins here,
that something that ACC doesn't have. And as well we can switch different types of view on the
viewer to see exactly in a way how we want to see it. The same we have as well for the issues,
various similar things, but different usage. The push pins are the same as the ACC, but
the FBX layer gives some extra advantage to see the exact spatial reality that comes from the
mesh, mesh layer. Another feature is the art piece integration, so you can put your buildings to
context of a city. And that can be also helpful to, if you plan some extensions of your building to
see how they will fit to the surroundings. And now it's it's time to give my word back to Martin.
In Kiereslove and now we are going to take a look how I will have some actually works in practice.
So first of our use cases is a digitalization project for Armassuys
Immobilien, which is let's say state agency which takes care of all our
buildings in Switzerland. And this project is Kidbrook. It consists of 16 buildings and each
building has different types of plants. So we have DWG plants for some buildings which are
in German. And we have also for some older buildings, hand-drawn plants in French, which is quite a
mess. And it's very difficult to actually know what's inside and it's very difficult to work with
and Armassuys approached us if we can help them and we actually could. So we got their plants and
we created a IOM model with it. The IOM model contains 16 buildings. You can download it as IFCU
and you can take the IFC file and seamlessly import it in GIS, which is the absolute favorite feature
of the Armassuys because they have many proprietary GIS layers for their military purposes.
It consists also of the data set with the information which can be also downloaded and every
single room has C-416 colorization which describes the type of the surface.
So they have a really nice model which was actually very affordable and we are working with them to
create farther buildings. I recommend you to take a look at the video. We don't have time for

which was so kind and gave us the permission to present it. So thanks to IOM, we consolidated
diversed documentation into one data set. No survey needed and no specialized software or

School in Herzoginnauerach in Bayern. And this is the example of model which was created

So we have created one digital model in IOM. Of course we provide a super clean IFC file which
is built up based on the building smart standards. It's integrated the 3D scans for the
virtual room tour and it contains the whole data set with default and last-term attributes.
Again the coloring was done by based on Cia 416 but it can be also created with DIM 277
as an international standard. Again this is how it looks like. So you can see the app, you can see
the rooms and the relations between the digital twin and the method. Again we have a nice video
and I recommend you to check it out online on our webpage. Now there is not you want to tell us
a little bit more about the technical highlights. Thank you Martin. So I will describe one of the
technical highlights as well the key challenges that we face during the development and one of them
is the schemes that we had to change many times before we got them right. They are responsible for
model extraction and model extraction is quite complicated process during which we have to establish
what levels are how the building higher he works because it can be completely dynamic.
And the user can define his own schemes that will match to his workflow the best but of course for
the default ones that we ship with our software is the one that matches the standard IFC
clean architecture which as you can see on the right is the IFC project, IFC site, IFC building
the levels and the spaces. The dynamic mapping allows us to use any kind of IFC files that
doesn't respect this exact hierarchy and still be able to import them into IOM. The next thing is
the mesh layers the FBX files which allows us to give some additional
objective reality that would not be possible to see on the very simplified geometry models and
when defining the assets and you need to like let's say put the pushpin on exact
what are pie or I don't know extend wisher you can easily use the FBX layer to
pinpoint the exact location in the room. Another possibility is as well the DVG files
which allows you to use some measuring if you need to get the exact
dimensions of a specific room or thing about let's say merging two rooms together you can use this
layer of a DVG files that serves as a floor plans for any purpose really and as well this is not
exactly related to IOM only all our products runs on our own internal IOM framework and with this
software we started the CRUD approach so all our APIs now are using this
approach and it owes off a very rapid development so we can spawn new endpoints very quickly
and as well because all of those endpoints are following the very same patterns it's very good
for MCP servers and for first part the product because it's easy to understand how it works.
The technical challenges I already highlighted some of them before but to be more technical and
more specific the technical challenge in terms of working with ACC was getting the derivative
model metadata because we often got the translation job to be stuck it took over two hours to get
the derivative model properties so we finally had to come with the workaround and the workaround was
to find a manifest for the SQL database and normally directly read it and get the properties
zoomier than two hours. The next challenge was the model stacking because as you saw on the screenshots
we are putting multiple models into one we were and it came with the problems like how to fit
model onto each other when each of them can be in different scale and different coordinate system
and lastly the dynamic configurable metadata extraction and the schemes which I already mentioned.
So what's next for IFM?
We've quickly finished the issues and currently we are starting with the sensors.
In plan there is the Gaussian split because the FBX layer currently looks quite a bit like melted
it melted after some like nuclear bomb or I don't know how to describe it it simply doesn't
look that great so the Gaussian split is our new technology that we are trying that could solve
the issue that it doesn't look exactly great and of course after that in July we would like to
